questions.stem: A child with fever is refusing fluids and has a dry tongue. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Assess for dehydration and monitor intake and output
Reassure the parent that fever always causes this
Wait until the next meal to encourage fluids
Ignore the finding if the child is still playing

questions.rationale: Poor intake with fever can lead to dehydration, especially in children. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever can contribute, but assessment is still needed. - C Waiting can worsen fluid deficit. - D Playfulness does not rule out dehydration.

distractorRationales.b: "Reassure the parent that fever always causes this" misses the bedside risk because poor intake with fever can lead to dehydration, especially in children points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "assessment is still needed" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Assess for dehydration and monitor intake and output" fits the safer response to assessment is still needed.

questions.takeaway: Fever plus poor intake should prompt dehydration assessment.

questions.stem: After receiving an opioid, a patient is hard to arouse and has shallow respirations. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Assess airway and breathing immediately and prepare to intervene
Document the response and recheck later
Give another dose because the patient is uncomfortable
Encourage the patient to walk to wake up

questions.rationale: Sedation with shallow breathing after an opioid can indicate respiratory depression. The nurse must act quickly. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay is unsafe. - C More opioid could worsen the problem. - D Ambulation is not the immediate priority.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Document the response and recheck later" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Give another dose because the patient is uncomfortable" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "After receiving an opioid, a patient is hard to arouse and has shallow respirations" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Opioid sedation plus shallow breathing is a breathing emergency.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient on oxygen becomes more confused and starts removing the nasal cannula. What is the nurse's best interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be becoming hypoxic or acutely altered
The patient is improving because they are more active
Confusion is unrelated to oxygen needs
The nurse should leave the patient alone to avoid agitation

questions.rationale: Confusion with oxygen removal behavior can indicate hypoxia or another acute change in condition. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Agitation is not the same as improvement. - C Mental-status changes can reflect oxygenation problems. - D The patient needs reassessment, not abandonment.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A patient on oxygen becomes more confused and starts removing the nasal cannula" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because - C Mental-status changes can reflect oxygenation problems suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Confusion is unrelated to oxygen needs" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: "The nurse should leave the patient alone to avoid agitation" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "- C Mental-status changes can reflect oxygenation problems" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Behavioral change can be a sign of worsening oxygenation.

questions.stem: An older adult becomes confused, cool, and has a weak pulse after a GI bleed. What is the best bedside interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be unstable from decreased circulating volume
Confusion means the blood loss is not serious
A weak pulse is expected after every bowel problem
The bleeding is probably resolved because the patient is still awake

questions.rationale: Blood loss can reduce circulating volume and cause poor perfusion, confusion, and weak pulses. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Confusion can be a sign of poor perfusion. - C Weak pulse is not a bowel-specific normal finding. - D Awake status does not rule out instability.

distractorRationales.b: "Confusion means the blood loss is not serious" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Blood loss can reduce circulating volume and cause poor perfusion, confusion" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "A weak pulse is expected after every bowel problem" moves care toward reassurance before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Because bleeding plus confusion and weak pulse can mean unstable circulation suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "The bleeding is probably resolved because the patient is still awake" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Bleeding plus confusion and weak pulse can mean unstable circulation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient becomes slower to respond and has trouble keeping their eyes open. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Assess for an acute cause right away
Assume the patient is bored
Wait for the change to pass
Ignore it if the vitals are not extreme

questions.rationale: Slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Boredom does not explain an acute change. - C Waiting can miss deterioration. - D Vital signs can look acceptable while the patient worsens.

distractorRationales.b: Because slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment suggests neurologic deterioration, "Assume the patient is bored" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.c: "Wait for the change to pass" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Ignore it if the vitals are not extreme" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: Any sudden change in responsiveness deserves attention.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly has trouble finding words and seems confused. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Assess for a possible acute neurologic problem immediately
Ask the family to wait and see whether it passes
Offer the patient a snack first
Document that the patient is anxious

questions.rationale: Sudden language change and confusion can indicate stroke or another neurologic emergency. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Waiting is unsafe when a neurologic emergency is possible. - C Food does not replace a neuro assessment. - D Anxiety should not be assumed without assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Ask the family to wait and see whether it passes" moves care toward routine observation before neurologic safety assessment.
distractorRationales.c: Because sudden language change and confusion can indicate stroke or another neurologic emergency suggests neurologic deterioration, "Offer the patient a snack first" would not correct the active problem. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Assess for a possible acute neurologic problem immediately" fits the safer response to a patient suddenly has trouble finding words and seems confused.

questions.takeaway: Sudden language trouble needs immediate neurologic assessment.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" moves care toward routine observation before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: "The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home" misses the bedside risk because a client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: Because severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency suggests clinical safety risk, "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" would not correct the active problem. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before renal protection.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting.
distractorRationales.d: "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. The cue "sodium 128 mEq/L" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before teaching or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" misses the bedside risk because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" misses the bedside risk because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" moves care toward routine observation before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to a nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion" misses the bedside risk because chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chills, low back pain" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: "Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster" misses the bedside risk because c diff spore precautions infection points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Place the client on droplet precautions only" moves care toward routine care before infection or sepsis escalation.
distractorRationales.d: "Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel" misses the bedside risk because c diff spore precautions infection points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
